  1. Permian frac capacity has tightened from around 90 to 100 fleets running at the start of the year to around 70 at the end of October. The lower demand has impacted pricing, particularly in the diesel-only capable fleets (the lower end of the market).
  2. Management is not chasing low-margin work. They are instead preserving their equipment for a future Permian activity rebound. For 4Q25, management plans to maintain activity at the 10 to 11 fleet level.
  3. Management is not ordering more e-fleets at this time. They like it, but they need better visibility into customer plans before committing to more capacity there. We noted Patterson-UTI (PTEN), on their call, expressing similar thoughts regarding the commitment involved with e-fleets relative to other technologies.

Power Update (PROPWR): Rapid expansion in progress

ProPetrol announced they have 360 MW on order vs. last quarter's 220 MW. Deliveries of the original 220 MW began in the summer and remain on schedule to be entirely deployed by the middle of 2026, with at least 220 MW under contract by the end of 2025.

Our Favorite Quotes From The Q3 Call:

  • "Deployment of our first assets in the field where we have observed excellent operational efficiency and reliability."
  • "We are actively negotiating additional long-term contracts. The demand for reliable, low-emission power solutions is accelerating, and we believe we are well-positioned to capture this opportunity."
  • "Given the accelerating demand for power, our active commercial pipeline and the expansion and extension opportunities available with our existing customers, we believe we are poised to deepen existing relationships, expand our reach to new partners and drive substantial long-term growth."
  • "We currently estimate that the total cost of this equipment, including the balance of plant, will average approximately $1.1 million per megawatt."

We see all of that as welcome and note that the all-in capital number should be viewed as a good price.

Deals announced and publicly disclosed in progress so far

  • Deal #1 - Permian E&P for 80 MW, 10-year contract, announced in May 2025.
  • Deal #2 - Hyperscaler Data Center for 60 MW, announced in October just ahead of the 3Q25 call.
  • Deal #3 - E&P power support, announced with the 3Q25 report (about 10 MW).
  • Potential deal #4 - 70 MW in negotiations with a large E&P customer announced today, with deployment expected before YE25. This means we can expect another press release soon.
  • That leaves nearly 80 MW to be announced by year-end.
  • All 360 MW to be in place by early 2027, and we expect contracts in place ahead of delivery.

Looking further out, PUMP plans to have 750 MW delivered by early 2028 with plans to go to 1 GW or more by 2030. This is their current PROPWR long-term potential view.

ProPetro Capital Outlook

The 2025 capital budget range was tightened lower to a new range of $270-$290 mm vs an already reduced range of $270-$310 mm. This takes into account lower spending on frac and an upgrade for PROPR to $190 mm vs. the prior $170 mm estimate. In 2026, PROPWR-only capex is now expected to be $200-$250 mm vs prior $60 mm as they rapidly expand the segment.

On the subject of how to pay for that power segment, management notes:

"To support this growth, we have secured a letter of intent for a $350 million lease financing facility, with closing expected before the end of the year. This facility is designed to maximize our financial flexibility, enabling us to draw funds as needed to accelerate or scale PROPWR projects. We intend to exhibit discipline in how we utilize this facility, ensuring we preserve a healthy balance sheet while supporting our continued growth."

Note that they will pay as they go, and they will not draw down the facility in advance of orders.

Other Items

Balance Sheet: Net long-term debt to annualized 3Q25 EBITDA remains low at 0.1x. Excluding ProPetro, our frac group long-term net debt to annualized quarterly EBITDA had a median of 1.2x this quarter.

Return of capital:

  • Dividend: None at this time, and we do not expect one before 2027, and maybe longer if the valuation at that point is not higher. If it is not, we would expect any return of capital program to focus on share repurchases.
  • Buyback: Historically, they have been an aggressive buyer of their own shares, but this program is now largely on hold as they scale up PROPWR.

Z4 Numbers

Frac and other services (Frac+) are seen holding at the subdued 3Q levels in 4Q with "normal" seasonality and at least at that kind of stabilized level into early 2026. The 10 to 11 fleets they see as active in 4Q are now 70% long-term contracted. This helps from a number of angles (utilization, planning, opex and capex cost control, as well as earnings stability). If we think about that level just holding flat in 2026, we're at about a $140-$150 mm EBITDA run rate. This would be an overly conservative view of next year's Frac segment math. The Permian is America's oil basket at roughly 40% of U.S. oil output. Our sense is that a recovery begins by mid-2026 or Permian production will fall. We would expect a modest increase back to the 14 spread level as an average for 2027.

Turning to PROPWR: ProPetro previously had 220 MW on order, and they see having at least that much online by YE25. Orders have now been increased to 360 MW. They expect to have about 300 MW in place by YE26 (with the remainder online early 2027), and they expect to have about 500 MW in place by YE27 (this and further increases have not been ordered yet, but they are confident they can get the capacity). 2026 segment EBITDA should be around $90 mm (this approximates $0.3 mm per MW per year with some additional capacity coming online as 2026 progresses). In 2027, if we just take their guidance for MW in place at YE26 and again add in some incremental capacity, we ballpark 2027 segment EBITDA at $120 mm.

Combining the two segments just for our early read math and assuming no further Frac+ segment cost efficiency, we get EBITDA of $225 mm in 2026, climbing to $360 mm in 2027. While the Street is well below us at present in 2027, we have seen one analyst go to $330 mm for 2027 far and think more upgrades happen as progress is made in 2026. This could easily move higher with a more meaningful Frac+ segment recovery.

In A Nutshell

The quarter was strong in our view. Capex edged down on lower frac needs offsetting an increase in spending for power. The frac outlook was fine, or at least no worse than expected, and they are taking steps to increase earnings stability with more long-term contracts (long sought and long elusive in frac).

We expect PROPWR segment news to dominate near-term sentiment in the shares. We got a new announced contract this quarter, the promise of more contract announcements near-term, and a bigger-than-expected expansion in MW's on order.

We expect 2026 and 2027 estimates to slowly slip higher as more PROPWR news arrives. And we see the name starting to take on some of the aspects of other oilfield service names that have gone all-in on power. But by no means do we view the current valuation as adequately discounting their medium-term PROPWR segment potential, nor a potential 2H26 recovery in the Permian, which we think is probable.

We own shares in both our FLEX and INVEST portfolios.

data.title: Cytokinetics: Approval Catalyst In December - But Risk Reward Is Not Compelling
data.description:
Investment Overview

I last covered Cytokinetics, Incorporated (CYTK) for Seeking Alpha back in March 2023, shortly after the South San Francisco headquartered, muscular disease focused biotech reported that the Food and Drug Administration ("FDA") had rejected its heart failure drug candidate omecamtiv mecarbil for commercial approval.

The rejection was widely anticipated and did not have much effect on Cytokinetics' share price, as attention had already turned to a new candidate, aficamten. I noted in my last post:

the focus of Cytokinetics entire drug development pipeline is directed towards something called "sarcomere" - a "molecular structure found in skeletal and cardiac muscle that enables myocytes to contract and generate force."

Notice, however, the difference between omecamtiv mecarbil and Cytokinetics' other Phase 3 stage asset - Aficamten. Whilst OM is designed to activate cardiac myosin - part of the cytoskeletal structure of the cardiac sarcomere - Aficamten is designed to inhibit it.

Cardiac mysoin is responsible for converting chemical energy into a mechanical force, resulting in cardiac muscle contraction, and aficamten - according to an explanation in Cytokinetics' 10K submission:

reduces myocardial contractility by binding directly to cardiac myosin at a distinct and selective allosteric binding site, thereby preventing myosin from entering a force producing state.

That theoretically makes Aficamten well suited to the treatment of both obstructive and nonobstructive Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy ("HCM") - a disease that is diagnosed in 280k patients in the US, and, Cytokinetics believes, undiagnosed in a further 400k - 800k patients.

Fast forward two and a half years, and we are just over one month away from finding if Cytokinetics' New Drug Application ("NDA") for aficamten will be approved by the FDA - the Prescription Drug User Fee Act ("PDUFA") date, i.e., the decision date is set for December 26th.

Analysis - Aficamtem's Path To Potential Approval - Takeover Rumors, Funding, Data

In my March 2023 note I assigned stock a Hold rating with shares trading at a value of $40, although I suggested that:

Cytokinetics is one of those biotechs whose current market cap either severely undervalues the company - or severely overvalues it - and we will very likely find out which before the end of this year.

That proved to be accurate, as Cytokinetics' share price jumped in value from ~$32 to >$90 in December that year. The first catalyst was data shared by the company from its SEQUOIA-HCM pivotal Phase 3 study of aficamten - according to a press release:

The results of SEQUOIA-HCM show that treatment with aficamten significantly improved exercise capacity compared to placebo, increasing peak oxygen uptake (pVO2) measured by cardiopulmonary exercise testing ("CPET") by a least square mean difference (95% CI) of 1.74 (1.04 - 2.44) mL/kg/min (p=0.000002).

The treatment effect with aficamten was consistent across all prespecified subgroups reflective of patient baseline characteristics and treatment strategies, including patients receiving or not receiving background beta-blocker therapy.

Statistically significant (p<0.0001) and clinically meaningful improvements were also observed in all 10 prespecified secondary endpoints, including Kansas City Cardiomyopathy Questionnaire Clinical Summary Score. Aficamten was well-tolerated in SEQUOIA-HCM with an adverse event profile comparable to placebo.

Rumors began to circulate that Cytokinetics would be acquired - potentially by Swiss Pharma giant Novartis (NVS), or former partner on development of omecamtiv mecarbil, Amgen (AMGN), or Eli Lilly (LLY), while analysts speculated that aficamten could achieve ~$2.6bn in peak annual sales.

In 2020, Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY) had paid ~$13bn to acquire Myokardia and its heart disease candidate mavacamten, which has subsequently gained approval to treat obstructive HCM, brand name Camzyos, which has earned ~$715m revenues in the first three quarters of 2025.

In the end, no deal materialized for Cytokinetics, and shares dropped back <$50 in May, as the company announced it had struck a deal with Royalty Pharma (RPRX) to provide $250m of funding upfront, and up to a total of $575m "to support the company’s further maturation and corporate development," and also announced a share offering to raise $500m, at $51 per share.

In November 2024, Cytokinetics announced it would partner with German Pharma Bayer to develop and commercialize aficamten in Japan, receiving a €50M upfront payment, and up to €490M in commercial milestone payments, and in December, an NDA for the drug was accepted by the FDA, who originally assigned a PDUFA date of September 26th. A marketing application for the drug was also accepted by EU regulators.

With takeover speculation rumbling on, but no buyer emerging, and with a lengthy wait for an approval decision, Cytokinetics stock lost further value, falling to a price of just $30 in May 2025, after the FDA communicated it had extended its review period for aficamten by three months, to December 2025. The FDA noted it required additional time to review the company’s newly submitted safety plan, known as Risk Evaluation and Mitigation Strategy (REMS).

More recently, however, shares have picked up in value - at the end of August they jumped from ~$35, to ~$50, as Cytokinetics shared detailed data from its MAPLE-HCM study of aficamten compared to standard-of-care beta blocker metoprolol, which met its primary endpoint by showing superiority in terms of improving exercise capacity in HCM patients, and also showing superiority in superiority to metoprolol in five of six secondary endpoints.

In September, Cytokinetics raised yet more funding - $650m of convertible notes, maturing October 2031. In November, the company announced its Q3 earnings and business updates, reporting $1.25bn in cash, a quarterly net loss of $(306.2m), or $(2.55) per share, $900m of convertible notes, and a year to date net loss of $(602m), or $(5.04) per share.

Analysis - Which Way Will The Dice Fall On December 26th?

Given how long Cytokinetics has been developing aficamten and its significance to the company (current pipeline shown below, slide taken from a corporate presentation), its hard to overstate the importance of the upcoming PDUFA date for shareholders and investors.

As we can see below, management very much sees its drug as a "pipeline in a product." Approval in the U.S. could be followed by approvals in the EU, and China - French Pharma Sanofi (SNY) is a partner in the region - approval as a monotherapy, and label expansions into, e.g., heart failure - a market in which the drug could match the likes of AstraZeneca (AZN) Farxiga, which earned $7.7bn of revenues last year.

Seemingly, the performance of aficamten in its clinical studies is regarded as as impressive as mavacamten's. According to an abstract I found on the Journal of the American Heart Association ("JAHA") website:

Both drugs demonstrated substantial benefit in reducing left ventricular outflow tract obstruction (Valsalva left ventricular outflow tract gradients improved by −45 mm Hg or better), symptom burden (placebo‐corrected New York Heart Association class improvement ≥1 of at least 30%), and cardiac biomarkers (geometric mean ratio of 0.2 for N‐terminal pro‐B‐type natriuretic peptide) while improving exercise parameters (improved placebo‐corrected peak oxygen consumption of at least 1.4 to 1.8 mL/kg per minute) in patients with oHCM.

Both drugs were generally well‐tolerated, although patients on mavacamten had higher rates of treatment interruption (partly protocol‐driven, 8.7% versus 0.5%, respectively, in oHCM) due to left ventricular ejection fraction reduction, atrial fibrillation (11.5 versus 4.1 per 100 patient‐years, respectively, in oHCM), and heart failure (1.7 versus 0.0 per 100 patient‐years, respectively, in oHCM) compared with aficamten.

If anything, aficamten is believed to have a slight edge on safety, although if approved, aficamten will have a REMS program, as Camzyos does.

On its Q3 earnings call with analysts, however, Cytokinetics' CEO Robert Blum told analysts, in relation to the progress being made agreeing on a REMS program with the FDA:

So we're continuing with interactions with FDA, and we have not finalized those matters. We do anticipate that we're making progress towards enablement of finalization of those in order to meet the PDUFA date. With that said, we've had exchanges and interactions -- and as I've indicated previously, we don't believe that we're engaging around framework, but rather some operational details, things that speak more to things like web pages and that which is administrative. Those are things that we think should come together to be enabling of FDA to review this and hopefully approve it in time for the PDUFA date.

Potentially, it may be a slight concern that discussions remain ongoing so close to the approval deadline, especially given the wholesale changes that have occurred at the FDA in 2025, as a result of the appointment of Secretary Robert F Kennedy to lead the Department of Health and Human Services ("HHS"), which has led to an overhaul of policy and leadership at the administration.

My feeling however is that Cytokinetics stock - trading at $66 per share at the time of writing, which implies a market cap valuation of $8.1bn - is priced for success i.e. the market believes aficamten will be approved.

As such I would not anticipate a major share price spike on approval, but if the FDA rejects the drug for any reason I'd forecast heavy share price losses. Given the number of agreements struck with partners, commercial preparations, and data shared to date, it would certainly be surprising if the FDA did not approve aficamten, in my view, but the administration has certainly done stranger things than that in 2025 to date, so investors should expect the unexpected.

Commercially, I'd point to the relatively slow start Camzyos has had - revenues of $602m in 2024, and ~$750m year-to-date in 2025 - and question if that is sufficient to support a bull thesis for Cytokinetics stock?

Cytokinetics has suggested it will price aficamten more or less at parity with Camzyos, if approved, and the company has plenty of cash to deploy for a commercial launch, with detailed plans already in place, and a commercial team that is launch ready.

Concluding Thoughts - Is Cytokinetics A Buy Ahead Of A Momentous Catalyst?

The December PDUFA is not the only catalyst upcoming for Cytokinetics - the company is still running a confirmatory Phase 3 clinical trial of omecamtiv mecarbil in patients with symptomatic heart failure with severely reduced ejection fraction, and a Phase 2 clinical trial of ulacamten in patients with symptomatic heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF), and a pivotal Phase 3 clinical trial of aficamten in patients with non-obstructive HCM, with results due in Q2 2026.

My take, ultimately, is that while I'd be fairly confident of a December approval for aficamten, I don't find the risk reward compelling enough to make Cytokinetics stock a Buy today. I feel as though success is mostly priced into the share price already, and note that former highs achieved of >$90 per share seem to have mainly driven by takeover rumors, which no longer seem to be circulating.

The fact Cytokinetics is going it alone with a commercial launch could be interpreted as a red flag - why hasn't a Big Pharma made a bid for the company? - but that doesn't mean the company cannot be successful - witness Madrigal Pharmaceuticals and its MASH drug Rezdiffra, for example.

Commercial success with Rezdiffra has seen Madrigal obtain a near $13bn market cap - the same price BMY paid for Myokardia in 2020, to gain access to Camzyos. Arguably, that could be a target valuation for Cytokinetics on the back of a successful launch, but then again, Rezdiffra does not compete against a market incumbent in MASH, as aficamten will do against Camzyos, and, given Camzyos is a very long way from fulfilling BMY's $4bn peak annual revenues target, it may be questionable if BMY feels as though it got genuine value for money with its Myokardia deal.

All things considered then, and despite a 60% gain in its share price since my last Hold call, I am going to issue another one - for my money, investors buying today have marginally more to lose than gain, although I can certainly understand why the upcoming catalysts persuades some people to load up on Cytokinetics stock.

data.title: As Sentiment Shifts, Fundamental Momentum Will Outperform Price Momentum
data.description:
Recent weeks have featured a change in market sentiment. The formerly high-flying momentum names have pulled back substantially. At a broad level, we can look at the Invesco tech ETF (QQQ) to see the break in momentum as it dropped from $630 at the start of November to under $600.

The drop is more pronounced in certain individual names such as Bitcoin (BTC-USD) or Palantir (PLTR).

While the sell-off in high-valuation names has been largely indiscriminate so far, I think it is worth discerning between different types of momentum.

Momentum is an umbrella category of investments, but within it there are 2 distinctly different types:

  1. Fundamental momentum
  2. Price momentum

Both are characterized by high or very high earnings multiples along with upward movement of stock price in recent history. Different traders will have different lookback periods, but both kinds of momentum will feature a stock price that is up significantly in whatever the lookback window happens to be.

The difference is that fundamental momentum is predicated on proper valuation, while price momentum is agnostic to valuation.

Fundamental Momentum vs. Price Momentum

A fundamental momentum investment thesis acknowledges that the price-to-earnings multiple is high but believes there is enough growth in future earnings that the high valuation is justified. The analyst forecasts specific growth numbers coming from specific lines of business and can model or otherwise show that the growth justifies the valuation.

In contrast, price momentum is the belief that a stock will keep going up because it has been going up. Perhaps the investor has a broad idea of why they like a company, but much of the perceived strength is inferred from the upward movement in stock price.

Both can coexist in the same stocks.

NVIDIA (NVDA) undoubtedly has some fundamental momentum investors who are forecasting a growth rate sufficient to justify its high PE multiple.

NVDA also has some price momentum investors who are in it because the stock has performed so remarkably well.

These different groups are impacted differently as market sentiment shifts.

How market sentiment impacts fundamental and price momentum

Fundamental momentum is immune to market sentiment in the long run. The stock price could take a big dip if market sentiment turns negative, but eventually they will be rewarded if they are indeed correct about the company’s growth rate.

Fundamental momentum is not beholden to the opinions of others.

Price momentum, however, is entirely rooted in the opinions of others. The stock price has been going up because other people like it. If sentiment shifts, a price momentum investor will take the same hit as the fundamental momentum investor, but their thesis is entirely broken.

If the only reason to own a stock is because it was going up, there is no longer a reason to hold that stock once it has corrected. As such, price momentum investors are susceptible to getting shaken out regardless of the strength of the underlying company.

Has market sentiment truly shifted?

The price movement in November hints at the start of a sentiment shift. Every bull market has micro-corrections within it, so it is probably too early to tell if this is merely a micro-correction or a true popping of the AI bubble.

Such sentiment shifts usually result from underlying concerns. In this case it seems to be some combination of the following:

  • Uncertainty about financing of the vast capex involved in AI buildout
  • Uncertainty about the ultimate return on investment for this capex
  • Sustainability of the trillions of dollars of annual capex

So far it seems to mostly be a questioning of the above. There is not enough data to definitively point one way or the other. Much of the pullback so far is related to the idea that market pricing was so high on AI companies that it indicated a near certainty of passing the ROIC test (return on invested capital hitting a key threshold).

Now that the outcome is more questioned or no longer considered a given, valuation has to come back down to give proper credence to scenarios where there is a pullback in capex.

As more data arrives, the market is constantly reassessing the probabilities of each outcome. Recent data has been mostly negative for the AI stocks.

  • Open AI’s margins seem rather low, with the cost of running LLMs (large language models) potentially higher than the revenues they generate
  • Circular financing between the big AI players
  • Difficulty of obtaining enough electric power soon enough

These concepts, along with others, and the appearance of well-respected bears like Michael Burry seem to be what has turned sentiment negative.

Time will tell if this was the true inflection point.

If sentiment does turn sustainably negative on the high-flying AI portion of the market, I believe fundamental momentum will materially outperform price momentum.

Defined exit points versus sentiment-driven exit

Most bubbles are built on some underlying truth. The 2000 internet bubble was based on the internet, which indeed was remarkably successful. AI is extremely powerful and will undoubtedly be successful in some fashion.

In each case there are winners and losers. The key is differentiating between those that can attach themselves to the long-term success and those that are merely clinging on to hype.

A price momentum investor may have significant trouble making this differentiation. When the internet bubble popped, Amazon (AMZN) dropped along with all the junk. Thus, someone who is primarily focused on price would have been just as likely to sell out of Amazon as to sell out of Pets.com.

A fundamental momentum investor has a better chance of discerning the great businesses in the wreckage. If fundamentals for an individual company remain strong, the fundamental momentum investor may be able to identify it and hold even if the bubble collapses and takes the stock with it. A fundamental momentum investor’s exit is defined by the fundamental thesis being impaired, not by the market price dropping.

The take-home point

When the market is soaring, both kinds of momentum work. If/when the sentiment reverses, it behooves investors to really know what they own and have a solid fundamental justification for it. I fear price momentum investors may be the most harmed in the next adverse market.

data.title: Dutch Bros: Growth Remains Solid, But Shares Look Priced For FY2028
data.description:
Dutch Bros (BROS) is a rapidly growing drive-through coffee chain company. A double-digit top- and bottom-line growth story has lost its positive price momentum, indicating a 1% drop in stock price year-to-date.

Although the recent sell-off may appear appealing, the company trades at a 75x earnings multiple, representing a 4.6x premium valuation versus peers at 16x. Even if the company meets Wall Street estimates and reaches $1.60 in diluted EPS in FY2028, this would put the stock at a 32x earnings multiple, about a 2x premium versus the sector median.

This argues that the stock is likely to consolidate further. Unless the company unlocks a new revenue growth cycle, it seems the market may have priced in future growth in the stock price. This leaves little room for error for management, although its performance remains outstanding.

Therefore, I rate Dutch Bros a "Hold" with a $53 price target over the next year. This suggests a 4% upside possibility, yet further consolidation. I don't think the company will significantly outperform the benchmark in the near term, unless a substantial catalyst emerges.

FQ3 2025: Steady Top And Bottom Line Performance

On Nov. 5, the company delivered FQ3 2025 results. This was the 9th consecutive double-beat quarter, highlighting solid top- and bottom-line growth. However, the market reacted to the results with a 4% decline over the following trading day.

Dutch Bros posted roughly $424 million in revenue, a 25% increase on a year-over-year basis, and $10 million above what the market had anticipated. The bottom line arrived at $0.19 in diluted EPS, a double-digit surge versus $0.16 in diluted EPS in the same quarter last year, and $0.02 above analysts' estimates. Although the market reaction may appear surprising to a double-beat performance, an overly inflated multiple could be the reason behind the breather.

In the earnings presentation, Dutch Bros highlighted that it has achieved 14% growth in the company's operated shops, an increase from 645 to 759 on a year-over-year basis, and an 18% surge in franchised shops, reaching 322 versus 305 in the same quarter last year. The total number of coffee chain shops reached 1,081. The company is strengthening its brand, market position, and positioning for future growth.

On the downside, the company's operated shop margin has decreased over the past 12 months. It has declined from 29.5% to 27.8%, and this remains an area to monitor for long-term investors. If margins were to erode further, I don't think the market will be willing to pay a significant premium for the company's operations. This could also result in weaker-than-anticipated bottom-line performance, weighing on the stock price and sentiment.

On the upside, the company managed to increase company-operated same-shop sales by 7.4%, which appears to indicate a solid demand. This also implies that the company's top line is mainly driven by openings of the new coffee chains, yet solid mid-single-digit growth suggests a strong market position and consumer preferences favoring BROS' coffee shops. The systemwide same-shop sales rose by 5.7%.

To conclude, this was a solid quarterly performance for the company, indicating that the business is running on all cylinders. However, although the company managed to achieve a double-digit top- and bottom-line growth, there are some concerning areas to highlight.

The company's margins are eroding, and I believe this is mainly impacted by cost inflation tied to input and labor. In addition to this, although mid-single-digit top-line growth in terms of same-store sales may appear compelling, I am not sure whether this is sufficient to maintain such a premium valuation. If the economy enters a recession, there is a possibility of weakening performance; therefore, I think investors should remain cautious at these levels.

Dutch Bros Trades At A Premium Multiple; I Favor Consolidation

Dutch Bros trades at a forward P/E of approximately 75x, highlighting a 4.6x overvaluation versus peers at 16x. Although the chart below indicates that the coffee chain is used to trading at these levels, this remains a substantial premium, and I am not sure whether the company's performance justifies such an elevated valuation.

On a forward PEG (non-GAAP) of 2.33, the company appears to be trading at approximately a 43% premium to peers at 1.63. This supports a bearish stance. Moreover, the forward EV/Sales and Price/Sales ratios of 4.66 and 4.06, respectively, highlight a 3.5x overvaluation at the midpoint versus sector medians of 1.26 and 0.87.

This is important due to a few things. To begin with, the valuation metrics suggest that the market may have already priced in the future revenue growth. This means that if BROS decelerates top-line performance or even maintains a high double-digit revenue growth pace, it may not necessarily translate to significant stock price appreciation. Furthermore, this argues for a significant premium, and if sentiment changes in the market, Dutch Bros would likely correct to closer-to-market valuations. It does not mean the stock can't trade at these levels, but investors should remain cautious.

The company has a moderately leveraged capital structure, implying $1.04 billion in total obligations and $268 million in cash. This resulted in $27.6 million in interest expenses on a TTM basis, which remains an elevated financial burden. This may partially cloud the positive outlook, as interest expenses weigh on profitability and bottom-line growth.

A significant top- and bottom-line expansion remains a key trait fueling a bullish narrative. Over the past year, Dutch Bros achieved a 29% growth on a year-over-year basis, representing a 9x outperformance versus peers at 3%. The bottom line grew by nearly 57%, an impressive advantage versus the sector median of only 1% growth. This supports a positive outlook and justifies the company trading at a high valuation. However, based on the recent quarterly performance, comparable sales grew at a mid-single-digit pace, arguing that growth is mainly driven by newly opened coffee chains. If BROS reduces the expansion rate, it may meaningfully affect top-line growth; thus, this should remain an area to monitor.

On the downside, Dutch Bros operates at below-market margins, and I think that heavily contributes to the bearish narrative. The gross profit margin (TTM) of 26% suggests a 32% underperformance versus peers at 39%. The net income margin (TTM) of 4.04% implies a 7% disadvantage versus the sector median of 4.34%. I understand that the company remains top-line focused, driven by double-digit expansion and openings of new coffee shops, but this may weigh on the stock price.

Dutch Bros may appear to be an interesting opportunity for long-term investors seeking to diversify into a high-growth consumer discretionary name. The coffee chain company delivers a rapid, double-digit top and bottom line, continues opening new locations, and is franchising the brand. However, it also operates at below-market margins, remains moderately leveraged, and trades at a significant premium, which may cap future performance. This results in a mixed picture, thus leading to my Hold rating.

Wall Street estimates $0.68 in diluted EPS for FY2025 and $0.89 in diluted EPS for FY2026. This would present a continuation of a solid uptrend in the bottom-line performance. If the economy doesn't enter a recession, Dutch Bros appears to be well-positioned to achieve these targets. However, I think the stock may consolidate for a while, resulting in decreased earnings multiple.

Therefore, if we apply a 60x earnings multiple to the FY2026 EPS estimate of $0.89, we arrive at my price target of $53. This would represent about a 10% lower valuation due to consolidation, yet stock price appreciation is fueled by bottom-line growth. Nevertheless, this would argue for a 4% increase in the stock price over the next year, as I think investors may favor less expensive alternatives in the space. Although a higher surge in the stock price is possible, I am not sure whether Dutch Bros may experience a significant increase in multiple expansions, leading to the benchmark outperformance, without unlocking a new top- and bottom-line growth catalyst.

Main Risks And Concerns

There are risks and concerns tied to Dutch Bros that may impact the stock price performance in the short term.

To begin with, the company appears to be trading at a significant premium to both peers and the average company in the S&P 500. If sentiment significantly shifts or the economy enters a recession, Dutch Bros may correct. I doubt that the downside is protected, and BROS is not likely to sustain such a premium valuation if sentiment dramatically shifts. Based on my analysis, it also appears that the market may have run ahead of the company's fundamentals. It leaves little room for error.

The consumer discretionary industry is fierce, especially within the coffee shop landscape. There are multiple companies competing for the market share, such as Starbucks, and it is challenging to have a sticky customer base. If consumer preferences change, or other coffee shops attract BROS's customers, top-line growth may decelerate, and it could be challenging to justify the company's premium valuation. Furthermore, if inflation persists or consumer spending declines, the non-essential services may experience reduced demand. This would not be a favorable environment for consumer discretionary companies.

On the other hand, if none of the risks and concerns materialize, Dutch Bros may continue expanding its business. If it succeeds in maintaining a double-digit top and bottom line over the following quarters, investors may be willing to maintain an elevated earnings multiple.

Conclusion: Dutch Bros Appears To Be Too Expensive To Argue For A Significant Multiple Expansion

I think that although Dutch Bros may appear to be a compelling opportunity for long-term investors seeking a high-growth consumer discretionary name, at the current valuation, it is difficult to justify multiple expansion. The coffee company trades at roughly 4.6x overvaluation to peers, and that remains significant.

Dutch Bros experiences substantial demand, but the top-line growth is mainly driven by newly opened company-operated shops and franchised shops. The same-store sales achieved a mid-single-digit growth pace, and I am not sure whether it would be enough to maintain such an elevated premium if the macroeconomic outlook weakens. I think the company is likely to consolidate unless it unlocks a new revenue growth cycle, which boosts the stock higher.

Therefore, I rate Dutch Bros a "Hold" with a $53 price target over the next 12 months. This suggests 4% upside potential, but also broader market underperformance. 19 Wall Street analysts imply a $76 price target over the next year, suggesting a 51% upside possibility. Although BROS could achieve the target, I am not feeling that bullish. I believe the company is more likely to consolidate.

data.title: The Fed Is Unlikely To Lower Interest Rates In The December FOMC Meeting
data.description:
This week, the market was highly anticipating two key events. The first one was Nvidia's Q3 FY26 results. In my pre-earnings article on Nvidia, I was bullish going into earnings, although I warned that, similar to AMD, a beat and raise could not lead to an immediate bull run.

Well, it turns out that was the case, as the market sold the rip on Thursday.

In fact, compared to the closing on Wednesday, the closing price on Thursday was down 3%.

The other event was the release of the employment situation report for September by the BLS. As I initially anticipated in my last macro article, the unemployment rate ticked up (4.4%), even though the (nonfarm) payrolls came in above expectations.

From a fundamentals perspective, I have a high conviction that AI stocks have not deteriorated. Earnings are beating expectations, and growth figures are well in the double digits for both hyperscalers and neocloud players. I see the selloff as an overreaction. That said, I don't see any signs that suggest an immediate rebound to all-time highs. Below, I explain why.

Circularity Is Not Fake Demand

Let me first explain why I believe the markets are selling off despite AI players in the likes of Palantir, Nvidia, AMD, Microsoft, or Nebius obliterating the Street's top-line expectations.

On October 4, Bloomberg published an article titled: "Why Fears of a Trillion-Dollar AI Bubble Are Growing". Since then, I noticed two main trends:

First, the proliferation of the term AI bubble in the titles of many articles from major media outlets and even in the macro section of Seeking Alpha. Second, the chart below:

First, circularity in financing and partnerships doesn’t mean revenues are fake. Back in the 2010s, AWS, Microsoft, and Google all sold infrastructure to each other and to overlapping customers. In the earlier smartphone cycle, Apple, Qualcomm, and many app developers were all tightly intertwined. To me, the key question is whether there is end-user demand outside the loop?

Well, take a look at the chart below from a McKinsey study, particularly at the light blue line representing the usage of generative AI among businesses in the US.

The same report shows in which department AI adoption is taking place: technology, especially software engineering. If you have ever used Cursor or CodEx for vibe coding, you know by now how useful AI is to generate pretty much any app that you want with zero experience in coding (as a startup founder, I'm speaking from my own experience here).

Another factor that suggests strong AI adoption is the recent wave of layoffs from big tech firms. The Challenger, Gray & Christmas report cited artificial intelligence as a reason for the 48,400 job cuts for 2025. I argue that, as more corporations wake up (three years after the start of the AI boom), we could see this figure accelerating in the next years, especially in publicly traded companies, where the need to maximize the bottom line results is key in each quarter. The most notable recent wave of layoffs was at Amazon, after the company decided to cut 14,000 corporate roles while simultaneously ramping up AI investment.

Finally, the last earnings season was strong, especially in the information technology sector.

Once again, the sectors where AI is not easily implemented (consumer defensive, consumer discretionary, energy, or health care) are showing a significant underperformance compared to the sectors where there is AI traction (mainly the technology sector).

The sequential increase in EPS growth from Q2 to Q3 this year was 5 percentage points for the tech sector. If that figure doesn't show growth, you tell me what does in the comment section.

We Haven't Hit The Lows Yet: The Fed Meeting Could Be A Turning Point

Price action and fundamentals are not the same thing. That's applicable both on the upside (when price action is ahead of fundamentals) and on the downside (like now, when fundamentals are strong, but price action is not following through).

As shown above, a strong uptrend was broken yesterday. The next support level is 6% below today's level in the S&P 500.

From a sentiment perspective, I see a lot of bearishness.

I believe a good part of this downward pressure is driven by the low expectations of another rate cut from the Fed. The BLS will not release the October employment situation report, and the one for November was delayed to December 16, 2025. Normally, jobs reports are on the first Friday of the month, but this schedule has been altered because of the shutdown delays.

A few days ago, Richmond Fed President Tom Barkin emphasized that the Fed is operating with limited data, seeing inflation still above target and job growth slowing down, and that upcoming data will guide the decision for the December meeting. According to the CMS Group, the chance for another rate cut has drastically declined in the past month, now significantly below 50%.

Conclusion

I believe the disappointment after the BLS delayed the November payrolls report has led to increased concerns of a further rate cut in the December 10 FOMC meeting.

The Fed only has the September payroll data, which came in above expectations. That said, the unemployment rate in September increased 10 bps from August, which is the only positive spin that I can make of this situation.

With no October jobs report and November delayed after the FOMC meeting, I believe the downward pressure could persist until December 10 (at least). Based on price action alone, there is a 6% downward move until the next support in the S&P 500.

Given that the spectacular earnings from Nvidia (and guidance for FQ4) were not enough to lift the sentiment, I foresee a continuation of the recent sell-off in the broader markets.

The next FOMC meeting on December 10 could be a catalyst to reignite optimism. However, the chance for another rate cut has decreased substantially due to the delay of the November payrolls report. If the Fed doesn't cut rates in December, I believe we could be in for an unpleasant surprise. For now, I downgrade to a Hold, and I recommend monitoring the Consumer Price Index report, which will be released on December 10, 2025, at 8:30 a.m. Eastern Time (right before the FOMC meeting, usually at 2pm ET). If inflation spikes up, the chance for a rate cut could decrease substantially.
